explanation:
**Normal health values** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. They are guidelines only; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How It's Calculated

Normal <5.7%. Prediabetes 5.7-6.4%. Diabetes ≥6.5%.

This calculator applies the formula with your values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to unusual results.

## Normal Reference Values

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| SpO₂ (Oxygen Saturation) | 95-100% |
| Fasting Glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL Cholesterol | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL Cholesterol | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult with a qualified healthcare professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What is a normal A1c level?
faq.a: A normal HbA1c is below 5.7%. Between 5.7-6.4% indicates prediabetes. 6.5% or higher indicates diabetes diagnosis.

faq.q: How is A1c different from fasting glucose?
faq.a: A1c measures your average blood sugar over 2-3 months. Fasting glucose shows your blood sugar at one moment. A1c doesn't require fasting and is more stable.

faq.q: Can I use this calculator to self-diagnose diabetes?
faq.a: No. This is a reference tool only. Only a doctor can diagnose diabetes. Always confirm results with a blood test and healthcare provider.

faq.q: Does A1c differ between men and women?
faq.a: Standard A1c ranges are the same for both. However, individual factors like age, ethnicity, and health conditions affect your personal target.

faq.q: What can I do if my A1c is high?
faq.a: Talk to your doctor for a personalized plan. Common strategies include diet changes, exercise, weight management, and sometimes medication.
Yes. Steroids, some antidepressants, and other drugs can raise blood sugar. Tell your doctor all medications before A1c testing.

faq.q: How often should I get tested for A1c?
faq.a: If you have diabetes, every 3 months. If prediabetic, annually. If healthy with no risk factors, screening every 3 years is typical.

faq.q: What does prediabetes mean for my health?
faq.a: Prediabetes (A1c 5.7-6.4%) means high risk for type 2 diabetes. Lifestyle changes—diet, exercise, weight loss—can prevent or delay diabetes development.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
